content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660           322-5901              322-6441                322-6444

February 16, 1984


Phyllis Matteis
3545 Bell Road
Auburn, CA  95603

Re:  Advice Letter No. A-84-030

Dear Mrs. Matteis:

     Thank you for your letter concerning the Political Reform
Act of 1974.  This Act is administered by the Fair Political
Practices Commission.  The Act imposes certain financial
disclosure requirements on public officeholders and on
candidates for elective office; however, it does not (despite
the name of the Commission) impose standards of "fair political
practices."

     Senator Ray Johnson, as an incumbent legislator and
independent candidate, is subject to the same financial
disclosure requirements as any other legislator or legislative
candidate.  As an officeholder, he will have to file a personal
financial disclosure statement (a "statement of economic
interests") with the Commission on March 1, 1984.  A copy of
this statement can be obtained by writing or calling the
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division of the Commission
after March 1 (916/322-5662).  As a candidate, he must also file
campaign disclosure statements.  His last statement should have
been filed on or before January 31, 1984.  His next statement is
due on March 22, 1984.  Copies of these statements can be
obtained from the office of the Secretary of State in
Sacramento, from the Registrar of Voters in San Francisco or
Los Angeles, or from the Registrar in his county of residence
(Butte County).

     You also asked whether it is a "fair political practice" for
a candidate to advertise that individuals who purchased tickets
to a candidate's dinner are "supporters" of that candidate.  The
Political Reform Act does not impose any requirements or
limitations on the contents of political advertisements, and it

Phyllis Matteis
February 16, 1984
Page 2


contains no provisions relating to who is or is not a
"supporter" of a candidate.

     I hope that this has answered your questions.  If you have
any additional questions about the Political Reform Act, the
Commission's Legal or Technical Assistance and Analysis
Divisions will be happy to assist you.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [signature]
                                        Dan Stanford
                                        Chairman

DS:BAM:plh

Dan Stanford, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street Mall
Sacramento, CA. 95814

Dear Mr. Stanford;

Please give me a lesson in "Fair Political Practices" regarding
an Independent who cannot appear on a June Primary Ballot, but who
launches a re-election campaign, none-the-less.

Enclosed is a copy of Ray Johnson's political ad for his formal
"kick off". My question is, "With this announcement, is Senator Ray
Johnson subject to the same financial reporting rule as are the
partisan candidates? If so, can you furnish me with copies of his
disclosure affidavits? If not, why not?

Because of my very vocal objections to the obvious Johnson
support from the ranks of Republican notables that are listed in the
ad, I have been told by friends of theirs that they were not aware
that they would be listed as supporters when they purchased a ticket
for this affair. If this was, indeed, the case, is it a "Fair Political Practice"?

I am the President of the Auburn Area Republican Women, Fed. and
it is my aim to provide information to my members and to the members
of the other Republican Women groups within Senate District #1.

Therefore, I will appreciate any comments you can send me by
speedy return mail. Thank You!

Sincerely,

Phyllis Matteis
Phyllis Matteis (Mrs. Richard)
3545 Bell Rd.
Auburn, CA. 95603

CC/ Etta Beall, Director
District #3 - Rep. Women

Paid Political Advertisement

Johnson launches
re-election effort

Senator Ray Johnson will kick off his re-election campaign in Placer County at a dinner January 27 in Johnson Hall on the Placer County Fairgrounds in Roseville.

The event will begin with cocktails at 6 p.m. A steak dinner will follow at 7:30 p.m. B. T. Collins, former director of the California Conservation Corps and colorful Republican chief of staff to former Governor Jerry Brown, will be the master of ceremonies.

Come join with us in saluting Senator Ray Johnson, our independent voice in Sacramento:

Harold T. "Bizz" Johnson, Mgr. James Corcoran, Bob Mahan, Alex Ferreira, Terry Cook, Richard Roccucci, Martha Riley, George Buljan, June Wanish, Will Linton, Pete Hill, Clarke Dominguez, George Wofford, Dan Higgins, Bud Pisarek, Henry Gonzales, Richard Azevedo, Paul Brocker,

California State Senator
Ray Johnson

Jack Parnell, Carole Onorato, Ross Biolo, Edwin Koster, Ray Yamasaki, Jack Shelley, Don Nunes, Al Nitta, James Bush, Frank Nutt, Carol Hamel, Mel Hamel, Mike Totaro, Joe Chevreau, Lyman Leak, Mick McCartney, Bob Noyes, Bob Flocchini, Bob Jimenez, John Carosecca.

For tickets contact Mahantucci's Roseville Liquor, 783-9200 or call 782-6965 or 786-5505.

Paid for by The Senator Ray Johnson Dinner Committee.

What loyalties?

Editor,

I'm regards to the recent paid political advertisement for Senator Ray Johnson and his up-coming steak dinner-fundraiser, I wonder how many readers noticed the list of sponsors. I found it interesting that there were many prominent local Democrats named as sponsors of a former Republican.

This leaves me with doubt as to where, if re-elected, Senator Johnson's loyalties will lie. In a legislature already controlled by Democrats, we need the strength of candidates who are firm in their convictions and work with and for their party. Senator Johnson abandoned the ideals and party he represented for years and is now engrossed in prolonging his own career and individual stature at the expense of all his former constituents.

It's no wonder the Democrats are anxious to pamper him.

Sincerely,

A disappointed Republican and former Johnson supporter

The above letter is not mine.
I sign all my stuff.

[illegible]

FOR YOUR INFORMATION...

Politics In Review

GOP's Party Purges

By Martin Smith, POLITICAL EDITOR

THE ATTEMPT to expel Roy Desmond from the Republican State Central Committee has a significance which extends far beyond the immediate situation.

On the surface, it's comic. It gives the appearance of a ridiculous effort by some relatively obscure Republicans types who are unable to stomach the obvious but essentially harmless political activities of a YR political sandbox. There may be more to it, though, than meets the eye.

The ouster attempt has the earmarks of an indirect move by some other Republicans—Caspar Weinberger, Deskmejian and an effort to get back Brophy and Montgomery for the apparent control of the GOP gubernatorial primary over the choice of the party's New Right direction.

Brophy and Montgomery, who is a former state party chairman, stand accused by Desmond of the crime of supporting the re-election of the state Senate's only independent, Los Angeles Democrat Ray Johnson, over the likely Republican nominee, first-termer John Doolittle.

THE CHARGE against Brophy and Montgomery is that both know the reality of the situation, which is that Johnson was a longtime GOP stalwart before his party label shift. Other Republicans during the reapportionment process, John Burton's gerrymandering of Johnson did disapproved of Doolittle's hackers and anyone else who put party politics ahead of what he viewed as dead sense, as a result of the reapportionment games, his original supporters, the conservatives who launched out from under him and, in effect, presented to Doolittle as a gift.

Instead, Johnson simply moved his residence back into the redrawn district where he can run under his own independent registration. He'll be competing as an independent in the November election. He's running against both the anticipated GOP nominee, Doolittle, and whomever the Democrats nominate in June.

Brophy and Montgomery are among the Republicans who have made it plain they think that Johnson is still the dream of that crop. They believe Doolittle, to say the least, he's no longer bearing the GOP label.

THAT SUPPORT may have provided the occasion for an early skirmish in the broader struggle for control of the California Republican Party in the post-Ronald Reagan era. It came immediately after November's general elections when, even if the same people remain technically in charge, the party will be a lame duck and the leadership of both the state and national scene will be in question.

Deskmejian clearly thinks he is governor, should be anointed as Reagan's heir apparent by those who identify more with the New Right place of the party as his friends. At a press conference last week, the governor, when asked about a possible Doolittle or Johnson, indicated both are acceptable to him.

By his reply, the five gentlemen and friends of mine:

And he flatly opposed the expulsion effort by warning all Republican

"We're a minority party as it is," he noted. "We have nothing to do with the ouster effort. He's in the East now, doing what he. basically can do to campaign for the national party. California Republican leaders generally feel, however, that a businessman governor won't be a candidate for public office again.

THERE'S ALSO AN ironic side to the move against Montgomery. The state's assembly Republicans on the state GOP can State Central Committee members for violating party orthodoxy. Montgomery, who presided over the state GOP.

Commenting sardonically on the Republican State Central Committee, Montgomery said recently:

"Remember the old days when Earl Warren was the nominee of both parties?"

Montgomery went happily along with the rule change, which not only makes the California GOP more ideologically pure but makes it impossible for Republicans in the Warren tradition but can cause trouble even for mainstream conservative politicians like Deskmejian and Montgomery.

The Sacramento Bee

Locally owned and edited for 127 years
JAMES McCLATCHY, founder, 1857-1883
C.K. McCLATCHY, editor, president, 1883-1936
WALTER P. JONES, editor, 1934-1959
ROBERT T. SMITH, editor, 1959-1978

Vol. 355—No. 45,684
Sunday, April 8, 1984

Editorials

paid for by the Senator Say Johnson Campaign
PO Box 874, Marysville ID# 790-310
M.L. Johnson, treasurer

sections.question: 1) Does Senator Ray Johnson, as an incumbent legislator and independent candidate, have specific financial disclosure requirements? 2) Is it a 'fair political practice' for a candidate to advertise that individuals who purchased tickets to a candidate's dinner are 'supporters' of that candidate?
sections.conclusion: Senator Ray Johnson, as an officeholder, must file a personal financial disclosure statement (statement of economic interests) with the Commission on March 1, 1984. As a candidate, he must file campaign disclosure statements, with his last statement due January 31, 1984, and next statement due March 22, 1984. The Political Reform Act does not impose any requirements or limitations on the contents of political advertisements, and it contains no provisions relating to who is or is not a 'supporter' of a candidate.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks about the financial disclosure requirements applicable to Senator Ray Johnson as both an incumbent legislator and independent candidate, and whether it constitutes a fair political practice to advertise dinner ticket purchasers as candidate 'supporters.'
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Senator Johnson is subject to the same financial disclosure requirements as any other legislator and candidate, requiring filings on specific dates. The Political Reform Act does not regulate the contents of political advertisements or define what constitutes a candidate 'supporter,' so labeling dinner ticket purchasers as supporters is not prohibited by the Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes FPPC advice letter (A-84-030) as primary content, dated February 16, 1984, signed by Dan Stanford as Chairman. Supporting materials include a paid political advertisement for Senator Johnson's fundraiser dinner, a newspaper letter to editor questioning his loyalties, and a political analysis article, but these are context only. The formal advice letter clearly identifies the question and conclusion.

The Political Reform Act does not impose any requirements or limitations on the contents of political advertisements, and it contains no provisions relating to who is or is not a 'supporter' of a candidate.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises Phyllis Matteis on the disclosure requirements for Senator Ray Johnson as an incumbent legislator and independent candidate, and clarifies that the Political Reform Act does not regulate political advertisement content or restrict how campaigns may characterize supporters.
